units.title: Unit 1: Basics

lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: isiXhosa is famous for its three click sounds: c (dental), x (lateral), and q (palatal). Greet one person with "Molo" and a group with "Molweni".

exercises.prompt: How do you greet a group of people?

lessons.title: Yes, No & Politeness
lessons.culturalNote: "Enkosi" (thank you) is one of the most important words you'll learn. "Uxolo" means both "sorry" and "excuse me".

exercises.meaning: Excuse me, may I have water.

lessons.title: Family & Respect
lessons.culturalNote: As in Zulu, you show respect by addressing elders as "Tata" (father) or "Mama" (mother). Mandela was affectionately called "Tata" by the nation.

exercises.prompt: How do you respectfully address an older man?

exercises.meaning: This child is five years old.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–10
lessons.culturalNote: Like Zulu, Xhosa numbers change shape with the noun they count. These are common counting forms used by learners.

lessons.title: Food & Drink
lessons.culturalNote: Offering food and drink is a sign of welcome. "Ndicela amanzi" (please may I have water) is a polite, useful phrase.

exercises.meaning: Please may I have tea and bread.

lessons.title: Directions
lessons.culturalNote: Begin with "Uxolo" (excuse me) when asking for directions. "Ekunene" is right and "ekhohlo" is left.

units.canDo: I can greet people and use basic courtesies (hello, please, thank you, yes/no).

units.title: Unit 2: Conversation

lessons.title: Introducing Yourself
lessons.culturalNote: Xhosa greetings build trust quickly. 'Igama lam ngu...' means 'My name is...'. 'Ndiyavuya ukukwazi' is 'Nice to meet you'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “What is your name?”?

lessons.title: At the Shop
lessons.culturalNote: At the market or spaza, 'Yimalini?' (How much?) and 'Ndicela...' (Please / I request) go a long way.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–5
lessons.culturalNote: Xhosa numbers, like Zulu, change with what you count. These are common forms heard when counting items.

lessons.title: Family & Respect
lessons.culturalNote: Respect for elders is central. Address an older woman as 'mama' and an older man as 'tata', even if they are not your parents.

units.canDo: I can introduce myself, say where I'm from, shop, and ask for help.

units.title: Unit 3: Everyday Life

lessons.title: Days of the Week
lessons.culturalNote: The Xhosa week starts with uMvulo (Monday) and ends with iCawa (Sunday, which also means 'church'). Several names build on counting, like Zulu.

lessons.title: Colours
lessons.culturalNote: Colour words agree with the noun in Xhosa. 'Luhlaza' covers green and blue; add 'okwengca' (like grass) for green or 'okwesibhakabhaka' (like sky) for blue.

lessons.title: Feelings
lessons.culturalNote: Say how you feel with 'ndi-' (I). 'Ndilambile' (I'm hungry) and 'ndinxaniwe' (I'm thirsty) are everyday phrases — the 'nx' in nxaniwe is a click.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “I am tired”?

lessons.title: The Body
lessons.culturalNote: Body words show up in greetings about health. Note the classes: 'intloko' (head) and 'isandla' (hand) take different plural forms.

units.canDo: I can talk about days, colours, weather, feelings and the body.

units.title: Unit 4: Out & About

lessons.title: Time & Routine
lessons.culturalNote: Mark the day with 'kusasa' (morning) and 'ebusuku' (night). 'Namhlanje' (today), 'izolo' (yesterday) and 'ngomso' (tomorrow) anchor any plan.

lessons.title: Animals
lessons.culturalNote: Animals feature in Xhosa praise poetry (izibongo). 'Inkomo' (cattle) carries cultural weight in ceremonies, much as in Zulu.

lessons.title: Around Town
lessons.culturalNote: Find your way by asking 'Iphi...?' (Where is...?). 'Isikolo' (school) and 'isibhedlele' (hospital) are good landmarks to know.

lessons.title: Numbers 6–10
lessons.culturalNote: Higher numbers in Xhosa get longer: 'isithandathu' (six), 'isixhenxe' (seven). The 'x' in isixhenxe is a lateral click.

units.canDo: I can talk about time, animals, places in town, and getting help.

units.title: Unit 5: Home & Community

lessons.title: In the Home
lessons.culturalNote: 'Ikhaya' (home) means family and belonging in Xhosa culture. Learn the rooms and furniture to talk about daily life.

lessons.title: Clothing
lessons.culturalNote: Everyday clothes: 'ihempe' (shirt), 'izihlangu' (shoes). 'Umnqwazi' (hat) has a click — the 'nq'.

lessons.title: Nature & Places
lessons.culturalNote: From 'intaba' (mountain) to 'ulwandle' (sea), the Eastern Cape is full of natural beauty. 'Idolophu' (town) and 'ifama' (farm) name where people live.

lessons.title: Question Words
lessons.culturalNote: Ask away: 'Ngubani?' (Who?), 'Yintoni?' (What?), 'Phi?' (Where?). These open up real conversation.

units.canDo: I can describe my home, clothing and surroundings, and ask questions.

units.title: Unit 6: Saying More

lessons.title: Action Verbs
lessons.culturalNote: Useful 'uku-' verbs. 'Ukunceda' (to help) has a click — the 'nc'. Pair verbs with 'ndi-' to say what you do.

lessons.title: Fruit & Veg
lessons.culturalNote: Market staples: 'iapile' (apple), 'ibhanana' (banana), 'iorenji' (orange). 'Imifuno' means vegetables.

lessons.title: In the Kitchen
lessons.culturalNote: Table words: 'ipleyiti' (plate), 'ikomityi' (cup), 'icephe' (spoon — the 'c' is a click). 'Ityuwa' is salt; 'iswekile' is sugar.

lessons.title: Bigger Numbers
lessons.culturalNote: Beyond ten: 'amashumi amabini' (twenty), 'ikhulu' (hundred), 'iwaka' (thousand).

units.canDo: I can use action verbs, name food and kitchen items, and count higher.

units.title: Unit 7: Getting Around & People

lessons.title: Transport
lessons.culturalNote: On the move: 'imoto' (car), 'ibhasi' (bus), 'uloliwe' (train). 'Inqwelomoya' (airplane) has the 'nq' click.

lessons.title: People & Family
lessons.culturalNote: The wider family: 'umhlobo' (friend), 'umalume' (uncle), 'umzala' (cousin). 'Umyeni' is husband; 'inkosikazi' is wife.

lessons.title: Sports & Fun
lessons.culturalNote: 'Ibhola' (soccer/ball) and 'umculo' (music) are loved across the Eastern Cape. 'Ukudlala' is to play; 'ukucula' is to sing.

lessons.title: Helpful Phrases
lessons.culturalNote: Smooth any moment: 'Andiqondi' (I don't understand), 'Ndiyaxolisa' (I'm sorry), 'Ndicela uncede' (Please help me).

units.canDo: I can talk about transport, people and sport, and use helpful phrases.

lessons.title: Joining ideas
lessons.culturalNote: Link your sentences: 'Ndilambile kodwa ndiphilile' (I'm hungry but I'm well).

lessons.title: Saying Where
lessons.culturalNote: isiXhosa folds 'at/in' into the noun: e- wraps the word and the ending shifts — isikolo (school) becomes esikolweni (at school).

lessons.title: Core Verbs
lessons.culturalNote: These stems power the frames you know: ndi- + sebenza → Ndiyasebenza. One stem, every subject and tense.

lessons.title: Weather
lessons.culturalNote: Weather small talk opens most conversations. 'Kushushu!' on a hot day needs no reply beyond a nod.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “it is cold”?

lessons.title: How & How Often
lessons.culturalNote: Adverbs follow the verb: Uthetha kakuhle — you speak well. ka- builds 'how' words: kakuhle (well), kakubi (badly).

lessons.title: People & Community
lessons.culturalNote: umntu (a person) → abantu (people). 'Umntu ngumntu ngabantu' — a person is a person through other people — is the heart of ubuntu.

lessons.title: Seasons & The Year
lessons.culturalNote: Seasons take the locative like places do: ebusika — in winter. The Eastern Cape's winters are famously windy.

lessons.title: The Body II
lessons.culturalNote: Body words power everyday care: 'Isisu sam sibuhlungu' — my stomach is sore — is the first sentence any clinic hears.

reading.title: Molo, Lindiwe!
reading.intro: Two friends greet each other. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.en: Hello Lindiwe. My name is Ayanda.
I am well, thank you. And you?
I am also well. Go well!

reading.title: Molo, Thandi!
reading.intro: Two friends greet each other. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.en: Hello Thandi. My name is Sabelo.
I am well, thank you. And you?
I am also well. Stay well!

grammar.title: Saying I, you, we
grammar.tip: Xhosa verbs begin with a subject prefix: ndi- (I), u- (you), si- (we), ba- (they). From -philile (be well): ndiphilile (I'm well), uphilile (you're well), siphilile (we're well).

grammar.title: One and many (plurals)
grammar.tip: Xhosa nouns change prefix for the plural: um- becomes aba-, isi- becomes izi-, in- becomes izin-.

options.why: 'Ewe' means 'yes'. Thandi greeted you — greet back with 'Molo!'

turns.speaker: you
turns.prompt: Say you're well and ask back

options.why: That asks 'how much?' — she asked how you ARE. Say 'Ndiphilile' (I'm well).
'Ndilambile' says you're hungry. The reply that fits here is 'Ndiphilile, wena?' (I'm well, and you?) — or 'Sala kakuhle' when leaving.
That says where you're from — she asked your NAME. Use 'Igama lam ngu…'
'Enkosi' is 'thank you'. She asked your name — 'Igama lam ngu…'
'Molo' is hello — but you're leaving! Say 'Sala kakuhle' (stay well).
'Ndilambile' says you're hungry. The reply that fits here is 'Ndiphilile, wena?' (I'm well, and you?) — or 'Sala kakuhle' when leaving.
